661:                 const item = btn.closest('.set-item');
662:                 item.querySelector('.set-name-display').classList.add('hidden');
663:                 item.querySelector('.set-name-input').classList.remove('hidden');
664:                 btn.classList.add('hidden');
665:                 item.querySelector('.save-set-btn').classList.remove('hidden');
666:                 item.querySelector('.cancel-set-btn').classList.remove('hidden');
667:                 item.querySelector('.delete-set-btn').classList.add('hidden');
668:                 item.querySelector('.set-name-input').focus();
669:             });
670:         });
671: 
672:         document.querySelectorAll('.cancel-set-btn').forEach(btn => {
673:             btn.addEventListener('click', () => {
674:                 const item = btn.closest('.set-item');
675:                 item.querySelector('.set-name-display').classList.remove('hidden');
676:                 item.querySelector('.set-name-input').classList.add('hidden');
677:                 item.querySelector('.edit-set-btn').classList.remove('hidden');
678:                 btn.classList.add('hidden');
679:                 item.querySelector('.save-set-btn').classList.add('hidden');
680:                 item.querySelector('.cancel-set-btn').classList.add('hidden');
681:                 item.querySelector('.delete-set-btn').classList.remove('hidden');
682:                 item.querySelector('.set-name-input').value = item.querySelector('.set-name-display').textContent.trim();
683:             });
684:         });
685: 
686:         document.querySelectorAll('.save-set-btn').forEach(btn => {
687:             btn.addEventListener('click', async () => {
688:                 const item = btn.closest('.set-item');
689:                 const id = parseInt(item.dataset.id);
690:                 const name = item.querySelector('.set-name-input').value.trim();

692:                 const response = await fetch('admin_cards.php?action=update_set', {

699:                     await refreshSets();

728:         const response = await fetch('admin_cards.php?action=get_sets&t=' + Date.now(), {

740:                     editSetId.innerHTML += `<option value="${set.id}">${escapeHtml(set.name)}</option>`;
741:                 });
742:                 if (currentEditVal) editSetId.value = currentEditVal;
743:             } else {
744:                 data.sets.forEach(set => {
745:                     setSelector.innerHTML += `<option value="${set.id}">${escapeHtml(set.name)}</option>`;
746:                 });
747:             }
748:             if (currentVal) setSelector.value = currentVal;
749:         }
750:     }

790:         if (e.ctrlKey || e.metaKey || e.altKey) return;
791:         if (e.key === 's' || e.key === 'S') saveCard();
792:         if (e.key === 'n' || e.key === 'N') { e.preventDefault(); newCard(); }
793:         if (e.key === 'd' || e.key === 'D') deleteCard();
794:         if (e.key === 'r' || e.key === 'R') revertCard();

85:             echo json_encode(['success' => true]);
86:         } elseif ($action === 'get_users') {
87:             echo json_encode(['success' => true, 'users' => User::getAll()]);
88:         } elseif ($action === 'create_user') {
89:             $data = json_decode(file_get_contents('php://input'), true);
90:             $username = trim($data['username'] ?? '');
91:             $password = $data['password'] ?? '';
92:             $isAdmin = !empty($data['is_admin']);
93:             $fullName = trim($data['full_name'] ?? '');
94:             $englishLevel = $data['english_level'] ?? 'Beginner';
95:             if ($username === '' || strlen($password) < 6) {
96:                 echo json_encode(['success' => false, 'error' => 'Username required, password min 6 chars']);
97:                 exit;
98:             }
99:             $user = User::create($username, $password, $isAdmin, $fullName, $englishLevel);
100:             echo json_encode(['success' => true, 'user' => $user]);
101:         } elseif ($action === 'update_user') {
102:             $data = json_decode(file_get_contents('php://input'), true);
103:             $userId = (int) ($data['id'] ?? 0);
104:             $username = trim($data['username'] ?? '');
105:             $fullName = trim($data['full_name'] ?? '');
106:             $englishLevel = $data['english_level'] ?? 'Beginner';
107:             $isAdmin = !empty($data['is_admin']);
108:             if ($userId <= 0 || $username === '') {
109:                 echo json_encode(['success' => false, 'error' => 'Invalid data']);
110:                 exit;
111:             }
112:             User::update($userId, $username, $fullName, $englishLevel, $isAdmin);
113:             echo json_encode(['success' => true]);
114:         } elseif ($action === 'reset_user_progress') {
115:             $userId = isset($_GET['user_id']) ? (int) $_GET['user_id'] : 0;
116:             if ($userId <= 0) {
117:                 echo json_encode(['success' => false, 'error' => 'Invalid user']);
118:                 exit;
119:             }
120:             Review::resetForUser($userId);
121:             User::resetProgress($userId);
122:             echo json_encode(['success' => true]);
123:         } elseif ($action === 'delete_user') {
124:             $userId = isset($_GET['user_id']) ? (int) $_GET['user_id'] : 0;
125:             if ($userId === $currentUser['id']) {
126:                 echo json_encode(['success' => false, 'error' => 'Cannot delete yourself']);
127:                 exit;
128:             }
129:             User::delete($userId);
130:             echo json_encode(['success' => true]);
131:         } elseif ($action === 'create_set') {
132:             $data = json_decode(file_get_contents('php://input'), true);
133:             $name = trim($data['name'] ?? '');
134:             if ($name === '') {
135:                 echo json_encode(['success' => false, 'error' => 'Name is required']);
136:                 exit;
137:             }
138:             $id = CardSet::create($name);
139:             echo json_encode(['success' => true, 'id' => $id, 'name' => $name]);
140:         } elseif ($action === 'update_set') {
141:             $data = json_decode(file_get_contents('php://input'), true);
142:             $id = (int) ($data['id'] ?? 0);
143:             $name = trim($data['name'] ?? '');
144:             if ($id <= 0 || $name === '') {
145:                 echo json_encode(['success' => false, 'error' => 'Invalid data']);
146:                 exit;
147:             }
148:             CardSet::update($id, $name);
149:             echo json_encode(['success' => true]);
150:         } elseif ($action === 'delete_set') {
151:             $id = isset($_GET['set_id']) ? (int) $_GET['set_id'] : 0;
152:             if ($id <= 0) {
153:                 echo json_encode(['success' => false, 'error' => 'Invalid set']);
154:                 exit;
155:             }
156:             CardSet::delete($id);
157:             echo json_encode(['success' => true]);
158:         } elseif ($action === 'get_user_sets') {
159:             $userId = isset($_GET['user_id']) ? (int) $_GET['user_id'] : 0;
160:             $setIds = Review::getAccessibleSets($userId);
161:             echo json_encode(['success' => true, 'set_ids' => $setIds]);
162:         } elseif ($action === 'set_user_sets') {
163:             $data = json_decode(file_get_contents('php://input'), true);
164:             $userId = (int) ($data['user_id'] ?? 0);
165:             $setIds = array_map('intval', $data['set_ids'] ?? []);
166:             if ($userId <= 0) {
167:                 echo json_encode(['success' => false, 'error' => 'Invalid user']);
168:                 exit;
169:             }
170:             Review::setAccessibleSets($userId, $setIds);
171:             echo json_encode(['success' => true]);
172:         } else {
173:             echo json_encode(['success' => false, 'error' => 'Invalid action']);
174:         }
175:     } catch (Exception $e) {
176:         echo json_encode(['success' => false, 'error' => $e->getMessage()]);
177:     }
178: 
179:     exit;
180: }
181:

1: <?php
2: 
3: session_start();
4: 
5: $currentUser = $_SESSION['admin_user'] ?? null;

8:     header('HTTP/1.0 401 Unauthorized');
9:     echo 'Unauthorized';
10:     exit;
11: }
12: 
13: require_once __DIR__ . '/../src/Database.php';
14: require_once __DIR__ . '/../src/CardSet.php';
15: require_once __DIR__ . '/../src/Card.php';
16: 
17: $pdo = Database::getConnection();
18: $stmt = $pdo->query("
19:     SELECT c.id, c.set_id, c.title, c.pattern_type, c.level, c.question_text, c.content_data, s.name AS set_name
20:     FROM cards c
21:     LEFT JOIN card_sets s ON c.set_id = s.id
22:     ORDER BY s.name, c.id
23: ");
24: $cards = $stmt->fetchAll();
25: 
26: header('Content-Type: text/csv; charset=utf-8');
27: header('Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="cards_export.csv');
28: 
29: $out = fopen('php://output', 'w');
30: 
31: fputcsv($out, [
32:     'id', 'set', 'set_id', 'type', 'title', 'level',
33:     'question_text', 'definition', 'sentence',
34:     'opt1', 'opt2', 'opt3', 'opt4',
35:     'correct_answer', 'explanation',
36:     'example1', 'example2', 'example3', 'example4',
37:     'usage1', 'tip',
38:     'image_url', 'description', 'audio_url', 'prompt', 'transcript',
39: ]);
40: 
41: foreach ($cards as $card) {
42:     $cd = json_decode($card['content_data'], true) ?: [];
43:     $type = $card['pattern_type'];
44: 
45:     $row = [
46:         'id'             => $card['id'],
47:         'set'            => $card['set_name'] ?? '',
48:         'set_id'         => $card['set_id'],
49:         'type'           => $type,
50:         'title'          => $card['title'] ?? '',
51:         'level'          => $card['level'] ?? 'Beginner',
52:         'question_text'  => '',
53:         'definition'     => '',
54:         'sentence'       => '',
55:         'opt1'           => '', 'opt2' => '', 'opt3' => '', 'opt4' => '',
56:         'correct_answer' => '',
57:         'explanation'    => '',
58:         'example1'       => '', 'example2' => '', 'example3' => '', 'example4' => '',
59:         'usage1'         => '',
60:         'tip'            => '',
61:         'image_url'      => '',
62:         'description'    => '',
63:         'audio_url'      => '',
64:         'prompt'         => '',
65:         'transcript'     => '',
66:     ];
67: 
68:     if ($type === 'multiple_choice') {
69:         $options = $cd['options'] ?? [];
70:         $row['question_text'] = $cd['question_text'] ?? $card['question_text'] ?? '';
71:         for ($i = 0; $i < min(4, count($options)); $i++) {
72:             $row['opt' . ($i + 1)] = $options[$i];
73:         }
74:         $row['correct_answer'] = $cd['correct_index'] ?? 0;
75:         $row['explanation'] = $cd['explanation'] ?? '';
76:     } elseif ($type === 'gap_fill') {
77:         $row['sentence'] = $cd['sentence'] ?? '';
78:         $row['correct_answer'] = implode(',', $cd['correct_answers'] ?? ['answer']);
79:         $row['example1'] = $cd['example'] ?? '';
80:     } else {
81:         $row['definition'] = $cd['definition'] ?? '';
82:         $row['example1'] = $cd['example1a'] ?? $cd['example'] ?? '';
83:         if ($type === 'usage_cases') {
84:             $row['usage1'] = $cd['usage1'] ?? '';
85:             $row['tip'] = $cd['tip'] ?? '';
86:         }
87:         if ($type === 'deep_dive' || $type === 'formula_table') {
88:             $row['example2'] = !empty($cd['example']) && empty($row['example1']) ? '' : ($cd['example'] ?? '');
89:             $row['tip'] = $cd['tip'] ?? '';
90:         }
91:     }
92: 
93:     $row['image_url'] = $cd['image_url'] ?? '';
94:     $row['description'] = $cd['description'] ?? '';
95:     $row['audio_url'] = $cd['audio_url'] ?? '';
96:     $row['prompt'] = $cd['prompt'] ?? '';
97:     $row['transcript'] = $cd['transcript'] ?? '';
98: 
99:     fputcsv($out, $row);
100: }
101: 
102: fclose($out);

1: <?php
2: 
3: session_start();
4: 
5: header('Content-Type: application/json');
6: header('Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *');
7: header('Access-Control-Allow-Methods: POST, OPTIONS');
8: header('Access-Control-Allow-Headers: Content-Type');
9: 
10: require_once __DIR__ . '/../src/Database.php';
11: require_once __DIR__ . '/../src/CardSet.php';
12: require_once __DIR__ . '/../src/Card.php';
13: 
14: $currentUser = $_SESSION['admin_user'] ?? null;
15: $isAdmin = $currentUser !== null && ($currentUser['is_admin'] ?? false);
16: if (!$isAdmin) {
17:     echo json_encode(['success' => false, 'error' => 'Unauthorized']);
18:     exit;
19: }
20: 
21: if ($_SERVER['REQUEST_METHOD'] !== 'POST') {
22:     echo json_encode(['success' => false, 'error' => 'POST required']);
23:     exit;
24: }
25: 
26: try {
27:     if (!isset($_FILES['csv']) || $_FILES['csv']['error'] !== UPLOAD_ERR_OK) {
28:         echo json_encode(['success' => false, 'error' => 'CSV file required']);
29:         exit;
30:     }
31: 
32:     $handle = fopen($_FILES['csv']['tmp_name'], 'r');
33:     if (!$handle) {
34:         echo json_encode(['success' => false, 'error' => 'Cannot read file']);
35:         exit;
36:     }
37: 
38:     $header = fgetcsv($handle);
39:     if (!$header) {
40:         fclose($handle);
41:         echo json_encode(['success' => false, 'error' => 'Empty CSV']);
42:         exit;
43:     }
44: 
45:     $header = array_map('trim', $header);
46:     $colMap = array_flip($header);
47: 
48:     $required = ['type', 'title'];
49:     foreach ($required as $r) {
50:         if (!isset($colMap[$r])) {
51:             fclose($handle);
52:             echo json_encode(['success' => false, 'error' => "Missing column: $r"]);
53:             exit;
54:         }
55:     }
56: 
57:     $imported = 0;
58:     $errors = [];
59:     $rowNum = 1;
60: 
61:     while (($row = fgetcsv($handle)) !== false) {
62:         $rowNum++;
63:         $data = array_combine($header, array_pad($row, count($header), ''));
64: 
65:         $type = trim($data['type'] ?? '');
66:         $title = trim($data['title'] ?? '');
67:         $level = trim($data['level'] ?? '');
68:         $setName = trim($data['set'] ?? '');
69:         $setIdRaw = trim($data['set_id'] ?? '');
70: 
71:         if (empty($title) || empty($type)) continue;
72: 
73:         $validTypes = ['usage_cases', 'deep_dive', 'formula_table', 'multiple_choice', 'gap_fill'];
74:         if (!in_array($type, $validTypes)) {
75:             $errors[] = "Row $rowNum: Invalid type '$type'";
76:             continue;
77:         }
78: 
79:         $levelMap = [
80:             'beginner' => 'Beginner', 'a1' => 'Beginner', 'a2' => 'Beginner',
81:             'intermediate' => 'Intermediate', 'b1' => 'Intermediate', 'b2' => 'Intermediate',
82:             'advanced' => 'Advanced', 'c1' => 'Advanced', 'c2' => 'Advanced',
83:         ];
84:         $levelKey = strtolower(trim($level));
85:         $level = $levelMap[$levelKey] ?? 'Beginner';
86: 
87:         $setId = 0;
88:         if ($setIdRaw !== '' && is_numeric($setIdRaw) && (int)$setIdRaw > 0) {
89:             $setId = (int) $setIdRaw;
90:         }
91:         if (!empty($setName)) {
92:             $existing = CardSet::getIdByName($setName);
93:             if ($existing !== null) {
94:                 $setId = $existing;
95:             } elseif ($setId === 0) {
96:                 $setId = CardSet::create($setName);
97:             }
98:         }
99:         if ($setId === 0) {
100:             $setId = 1;
101:         }
102: 
103:         $questionText = trim($data['question_text'] ?? '');
104:         $definition = trim($data['definition'] ?? '');
105:         $sentence = trim($data['sentence'] ?? '');
106:         $correctAnswer = trim($data['correct_answer'] ?? '');
107:         $explanation = trim($data['explanation'] ?? '');
108:         $usage1 = trim($data['usage1'] ?? '');
109:         $tip = trim($data['tip'] ?? '');
110: 
111:         $examples = [];
112:         for ($i = 1; $i <= 4; $i++) {
113:             $val = trim($data["example$i"] ?? '');
114:             if (!empty($val)) $examples[] = $val;
115:         }
116: 
117:         $contentData = [];
118: 
119:         if ($type === 'multiple_choice') {
120:             $options = [];
121:             for ($i = 1; $i <= 4; $i++) {
122:                 $val = trim($data["opt$i"] ?? '');
123:                 if (!empty($val)) $options[] = $val;
124:             }
125:             if (empty($options)) {
126:                 $errors[] = "Row $rowNum: Multiple choice needs at least one option";
127:                 continue;
128:             }
129:             $correctIdx = 0;
130:             if ($correctAnswer !== '') {
131:                 $correctIdx = (int) $correctAnswer;
132:                 if ($correctIdx < 0 || $correctIdx >= count($options)) $correctIdx = 0;
133:             }
134:             $contentData = [
135:                 'options' => $options,
136:                 'correct_index' => $correctIdx,
137:                 'question_text' => $questionText ?: 'Select the correct answer:',
138:                 'explanation' => $explanation,
139:             ];
140:         } elseif ($type === 'gap_fill') {
141:             $answers = !empty($correctAnswer) ? array_map('trim', explode(',', $correctAnswer)) : ['answer'];
142:             $contentData = [
143:                 'sentence' => $sentence ?: 'Complete: ______',
144:                 'correct_answers' => $answers,
145:                 'example' => $examples[0] ?? '',
146:             ];
147:         } else {
148:             $contentData = [
149:                 'definition' => $definition ?: 'No definition',
150:             ];
151:             if (!empty($usage1)) $contentData['usage1'] = $usage1;
152:             if (!empty($examples)) $contentData['example1a'] = $examples[0];
153:             if (!empty($tip)) $contentData['tip'] = $tip;
154:             if (count($examples) > 1) $contentData['example'] = $examples[1] ?? $examples[0];
155:         }
156: 
157:         if ($type === 'usage_cases' && empty($usage1) && !empty($examples)) {
158:             $contentData['usage1'] = $examples[0];
159:         }
160: 
161:         $cardId = isset($data['id']) && is_numeric($data['id']) ? (int) $data['id'] : 0;
162: 
163:         Card::save([
164:             'id' => $cardId,
165:             'set_id' => $setId,
166:             'title' => $title,
167:             'pattern_type' => $type,
168:             'level' => $level,
169:             'question_text' => $type === 'multiple_choice' ? $questionText : '',
170:             'content_data' => $contentData,
171:         ]);
172:         $imported++;
173:     }
174: 
175:     fclose($handle);
176: 
177:     echo json_encode([
178:         'success' => true,
179:         'imported' => $imported,
180:         'errors' => $errors,
181:     ]);
182: } catch (Exception $e) {
183:     echo json_encode([
184:         'success' => false,
185:         'error' => $e->getMessage(),
186:     ]);
187: }

(End of file - total 187 lines)
</content>
```

2. Audio card: plays audio

And these come in two modes:
- Descriptive: just show/play the media with description
- QnA: show/play the media and ask a question

So the mapping is:
- image_mcq = picture + QnA (image + multiple choice)
- image_description = picture + descriptive (image + description)
- audio_listening = audio + QnA/descriptive (audio + prompt/transcription/interactive)

{
  "filePath": "/home/leonardo/Documents/Personal/Inglês/Aulas/materialLeo/ankiCards/flashcards/src/Card.php",
  "oldString": "    public static function delete(int $id): void\n    {\n        $pdo = Database::getConnection();\n        $stmt = $pdo->prepare(\"DELETE FROM cards WHERE id = ?\");\n        $stmt->execute([$id]);\n    }\n}",
  "newString": "    public static function delete(int $id): void\n    {\n        $pdo = Database::getConnection();\n        $stmt = $pdo->prepare(\"DELETE FROM cards WHERE id = ?\");\n        $stmt->execute([$id]);\n    }\n\n    private static function ensurePatternTypeEnum(string $type): void\n    {\n        $known = ['usage_cases','deep_dive','formula_table','multiple_choice','gap_fill','image_description','audio_listening','image_mcq'];\n        if (!in_array($type, $known)) return;\n\n        $pdo = Database::getConnection();\n        $col = $pdo->query(\"SHOW COLUMNS FROM cards WHERE Field = 'pattern_type'\")->fetch();\n        if (!$col) return;\n\n        $enum = $col['Type'];\n        if (strpos($enum, $type) !== false) return;\n\n        $escaped = array_map(fn($v) => \"'\" . str_replace(\"'\", \"''\", $v) . \"'\", $known);\n        $pdo->exec(\"ALTER TABLE cards MODIFY pattern_type ENUM(\" . implode(',', $escaped) . \") DEFAULT 'usage_cases'\");\n    }\n}"
}

text-gray-600 mt-1 p-2 bg-gray-50 rounded-lg\">📝 ${explanation}</div>` : ''}\n                        <p class=\"text-xs text-gray-400 mt-2\">Rate your recall using the buttons below</p>\n                    </div>\n                </div>\n            `;\n        } else if (pattern === 'image_description') {\n            const description = formatBreaks(escapeHtml(data.description || 'No description'));\n            return `\n                <div class=\"back-content\">\n                    <div class=\"text-center\">\n                        <h3 class=\"text-lg text-blue-700 title-font marker-underline mb-2\">${escapeHtml(title)}</h3>\n                        <div class=\"card-definition bg-blue-50 p-3 rounded-xl border-2 border-blue-300\">\n                            ${description}\n                        </div>\n                        <p class=\"text-xs text-gray-400 mt-2\">Rate your recall using the buttons below</p>\n                    </div>\n                </div>\n            `;\n        } else if (pattern === 'audio_listening') {\n            const transcript = formatBreaks(escapeHtml(data.transcript || data.notes || ''));\n            const correctAnswers = data.correct_answers || [];\n            const userAnswer = quizState?.userAnswer || '';\n            const isInteractive = !!(data.prompt || correctAnswers.length);\n            let isMatch = false;\n            if (userAnswer && correctAnswers.length) {\n                isMatch = correctAnswers.some(ans => ans.toLowerCase() === userAnswer.toLowerCase());\n            } else if (userAnswer && data.transcript) {\n                isMatch = data.transcript.toLowerCase().trim() === userAnswer.toLowerCase().trim();\n            }\n            return `\n                <div class=\"back-content\">\n                    <div class=\"text-center\">\n                        <h3 class=\"text-lg text-green-700 title-font marker-underline mb-2\">${escapeHtml(title)}</h3>\n                        ${transcript ? `<div class=\"card-definition bg-green-50 p-3 rounded-xl border-2 border-green-300 mb-2\">${transcript}</div>` : ''}\n                        ${isInteractive && userAnswer ? `\n                        <div class=\"p-2 rounded-lg mb-2 ${isMatch ? 'bg-green-100 text-green-800' : 'bg-red-100 text-red-800'}\">\n                            <p class=\"text-sm\">${isMatch ? '✅ Correct!' : `❌ Your answer: \"${escapeHtml(userAnswer)}\"`}</p>\n                        </div>\n                        ` : isInteractive && !userAnswer ? `<p class=\"text-sm text-gray-500 mb-2\">Type what you heard above, then flip.</p>` : ''}\n                        <p class=\"text-xs text-gray-400 mt-2\">Rate your recall using the buttons below</p>\n                    </div>\n                </div>\n            `;\n        } else {\n            const definition = formatBreaks(escapeHtml(data.definition || data.usage1 || 'No definition available'));\n            const example = formatBreaks(escapeHtml(data.example1a || data.example || ''));\n\n            return `\n                <div class=\"back-content\">\n                    <div class=\"text-center\">\n                        <h3 class=\"text-lg text-blue-700 title-font marker-underline mb-2\">${escapeHtml(title)}</h3>\n                        <div class=\"card-definition bg-blue-50 p-3 rounded-xl border-2 border-blue-300\">\n                            ${definition}\n                        </div>\n                        ${example ? `\n                            <div class=\"mt-2 p-2 bg-gray-100 rounded-lg\">\n                                <p class=\"text-sm\"><strong>Example:</strong> ${example}</p>\n                            </div>\n                        ` : ''}\n                        <p class=\"text-xs text-gray-400 mt-2\">Rate your recall using the buttons below</p>\n                    </div>\n                </div>\n            `;\n        }"
}

Done. Here's a summary of all changes:

## Changes Summary

### `src/Card.php`
- Added `ensurePatternTypeEnum()` — auto-extends the ENUM column when saving new pattern types (`image_mcq`, `image_description`, `audio_listening`) so existing DB doesn't silently truncate them.
- Called `self::ensurePatternTypeEnum($patternType)` in `save()` before INSERT/UPDATE.

### `assets/js/app.js`
- **`renderCardFront`**: Added branches for:
  - `image_mcq` — responsive side-by-side layout (`flex-col md:flex-row`), image left, MCQ options right
  - `image_description` — centered image with title above
  - `audio_listening` — audio player with optional prompt + text input
  - `gap_fill` — now also shows optional image/audio media on top
- **`renderCardBack`**: Added branches for all three new types (MCQ-style answer for image_mcq, description for image_description, transcript + correctness for audio_listening)
- **Flip handler**: Added `image_mcq` to MCQ selection branch, `audio_listening` to gap_fill input branch
- **Click handler**: Added `audio` element exclusion so clicking audio controls doesn't flip
- **Review logic**: Added `image_mcq` and `audio_listening` to the correctness check chain

### `assets/js/admin.js`
- **Card list**: Shows `ImgMCQ`, `Audio`, `Image` type labels
- **`renderEditFields`**: Added fields for `image_description` (image URL + description), `image_mcq` (image URL + question + options + correct index + explanation), `audio_listening` (audio URL + prompt + correct answers + notes). Also added image/audio URL fields to `multiple_choice`, `gap_fill`, and generic text types.
- **`getCurrentContentData`**: Extracts image_url, audio_url for all relevant types
- **`updatePreviews`**: Front/back previews for all new types, including media display in previews
- **`saveCard`**: Maps `question_text` for `image_mcq` the same as `multiple_choice`

### `admin_cards.php`
- Added `image_mcq`, `image_description`, `audio_listening` options to the pattern type `<select>` dropdown

### `api/export_csv.php`
- Added type-specific export logic for `image_mcq`, `image_description`, `audio_listening`
- Now also exports `notes` as fallback for `transcript`

### `api/import_csv.php`
- Added all three new types to `$validTypes`
- Added content_data construction for `image_mcq`, `image_description`, `audio_listening`
- Added image_url/audio_url fields to `multiple_choice`, `gap_fill`, and generic text imports
- Mapped `question_text` for `image_mcq`

(NOT: Which is your name)"",""example1b"":""What is your phone number?"",""usage2"":""Characteristics"",""example2"":""What colour is your car? What time is it?"",""teacher_note"":""In Portuguese you say 'Qual é seu nome?' but in English we say 'What is your name?' This is a very common mistake to avoid!""}"
4: 1,"WHO - Asking About People",formula_table,Beginner,"How to ask about people using WHO?","{""formula"":""Who + verb + complement?"",""affirmative"":""Who is your teacher?"",""negative"":""Who isn't coming?"",""question"":""Who called you?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Identity"",""aff"":""Who are you?"",""ques"":""Who is that woman?""},{""person"":""People in actions"",""aff"":""Who did this?"",""ques"":""Who called you?""},{""person"":""With prepositions"",""aff"":""Who do you live with?"",""ques"":""Who are you talking to?""}]}"
5: 1,"WHO - Prepositions at the End",deep_dive,Intermediate,"Why prepositions go at the end of WHO questions","{""bullets"":[""In English, prepositions (with, for, to, about) go at the END of WHO questions"",""❌ With who do you live? (too formal, unnatural)"",""✅ Who do you live with? (correct, natural)"",""❌ To who are you talking?"",""✅ Who are you talking to?"",""❌ For who is this gift?"",""✅ Who is this gift for?""],""explanation"":""Portuguese puts the preposition at the beginning ('Com quem você mora?'). English puts it at the end. This is one of the biggest differences between the two languages. Don't try to translate word-for-word!""}"
6: 1,"WHERE - Asking About Places",formula_table,Beginner,"How to ask about locations using WHERE?","{""formula"":""Where + be + subject? (location) OR Where + do/does + subject + main verb? (action in a place)"",""affirmative"":""Where is the supermarket?"",""negative"":""Where isn't she?"",""question"":""Where do you live?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Location"",""aff"":""Where are you?"",""ques"":""Where is the bathroom?""},{""person"":""Origin"",""aff"":""Where are you from?"",""ques"":""Where do you come from?""},{""person"":""Action location"",""aff"":""Where does she work?"",""ques"":""Where do you study?""}]}"
7: 1,"WHERE - The ""FROM"" Rule",usage_cases,Beginner,"Using WHERE with ""from"" to ask about origin","{""usage1"":""Asking where someone was born/raised"",""example1a"":""Where are you from? = De onde você é?"",""example1b"":""Where do you come from? = De onde você vem?"",""usage2"":""The preposition comes at the end"",""example2"":""NOT: From where are you? (too formal)"",""teacher_note"":""Just like Portuguese puts 'de' at the beginning ('De onde você é?'), English puts 'from' at the end. Same meaning, different word order!""}"
8: 1,"WHEN - Asking About Time",formula_table,Beginner,"How to ask about days, dates, hours using WHEN?","{""formula"":""When + be + subject? (fixed event) OR When + do/does + subject + main verb? (action time)"",""affirmative"":""When is your birthday?"",""negative"":""When don't you work?"",""question"":""When does the movie start?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Future time"",""aff"":""When is the party?"",""ques"":""When will you arrive?""},{""person"":""Past time"",""aff"":""When were you born?"",""ques"":""When did you arrive?""},{""person"":""Daily routines"",""aff"":""When do you wake up?"",""ques"":""When do you have lunch?""}]}"
9: 1,"WHEN - Answering with Time Expressions",usage_cases,Beginner,"How to answer WHEN questions","{""usage1"":""Specific times"",""example1a"":""At 7 o'clock / at 8:30"",""example1b"":""At noon / at midnight"",""usage2"":""Days and dates"",""example2"":""On Monday / on July 4th / on my birthday"",""usage3"":""Months and years"",""example3"":""In June / in 2024 / in summer"",""usage4"":""Relative time"",""example4"":""Yesterday / tomorrow / next week / two days ago""}"
10: 1,"WHY - Asking for Reasons",formula_table,Beginner,"How to ask for explanations using WHY?","{""formula"":""Why + do/does/did + subject + main verb? OR Why + be + subject + adjective?"",""affirmative"":""Why are you sad?"",""negative"":""Why aren't you coming?"",""question"":""Why do you study English?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Emotions"",""aff"":""Why are you happy?"",""ques"":""Why is she crying?""},{""person"":""Actions"",""aff"":""Why did you leave?"",""ques"":""Why do you work so hard?""},{""person"":""Explanations"",""aff"":""Why is the store closed?"",""ques"":""Why didn't you call?""}]}"
11: 1,"WHY - Answering with BECAUSE",usage_cases,Beginner,"How to answer WHY questions correctly","{""usage1"":""Standard answer structure"",""example1a"":""Why are you studying? → Because I have a test tomorrow."",""example1b"":""Why is she late? → Because the bus didn't come."",""usage2"":""BECAUSE vs BECAUSE OF"",""example2"":""Because + clause (subject + verb): Because I was tired."",""example2b"":""Because of + noun: Because of the traffic."",""teacher_note"":""In Portuguese, 'por que' (question) vs 'porque' (answer). English also has this difference: 'Why' asks, 'Because' answers!""}"
12: 1,"WHICH - Choosing from Limited Options",formula_table,Intermediate,"How to ask for a choice from a specific group","{""formula"":""Which + noun + verb? OR Which + verb? (when noun is understood)"",""affirmative"":""Which shirt do you prefer?"",""negative"":""Which one don't you like?"",""question"":""Which is your car?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Two options"",""aff"":""Which colour, black or white?"",""ques"":""Which hand do you write with?""},{""person"":""Multiple options"",""aff"":""Which of these books is yours?"",""ques"":""Which day works for you?""},{""person"":""Singular vs plural"",""aff"":""Which one? / Which ones?"",""ques"":""Which shoes are you wearing?""}]}"
13: 1,"WHAT vs WHICH - The Critical Difference",deep_dive,Intermediate,"When to use WHAT (open) vs WHICH (limited choices)","{""bullets"":[""WHAT = unlimited possibilities. The answer is open."",""WHICH = limited options. The answer comes from a small, specific group."",""WHAT example: 'What movie do you want to watch?' (any movie in the world)"",""WHICH example: 'Which movie do you want: Comedy or Horror?' (only two choices)"",""WHAT: 'What is your phone number?' (unique answer, general info)"",""WHICH: 'Which number is yours?' (looking at a list of 3 numbers)""],""explanation"":""If you can imagine a short list of options (2, 3, or 4 things), use WHICH. If you cannot imagine a short list because there are too many possibilities, use WHAT. This is different from Portuguese where 'qual' is used more often.""}"
14: 1,"WHOSE - Asking About Possession",formula_table,Beginner,"How to ask who owns something using WHOSE","{""formula"":""Whose + noun + be + ...?"",""affirmative"":""Whose phone is this?"",""negative"":""Whose car isn't working?"",""question"":""Whose books are these?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Single item"",""aff"":""Whose pen is this?"",""ques"":""Whose idea was it?""},{""person"":""Multiple items"",""aff"":""Whose keys are these?"",""ques"":""Whose children are they?""},{""person"":""Answer format"",""aff"":""It's my pen / It's mine"",""ques"":""It's hers / It's his""}]}"
15: 1,"WHOSE - Answering with Possessives",usage_cases,Beginner,"How to answer WHOSE questions using possessive adjectives and pronouns","{""usage1"":""Possessive adjectives (need a noun)"",""example1a"":""It's my pen. / It's your book. / It's his car."",""example1b"":""It's her phone. / It's our house. / It's their dog."",""usage2"":""Possessive pronouns (replace the noun)"",""example2a"":""It's mine. / It's yours. / It's his."",""example2b"":""It's hers. / It's ours. / It's theirs."",""teacher_note"":""Possessive pronouns are shorter and more natural in conversation. 'Whose phone is this?' → 'Mine.' (one word!)""}"
16: 1,"HOW - Manner and Condition",formula_table,Beginner,"Using HOW to ask about way, state, and condition","{""formula"":""How + be + subject? (condition) OR How + do/does + subject + main verb? (manner)"",""affirmative"":""How are you?"",""negative"":""How isn't it working?"",""question"":""How do you make pizza?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Condition/health"",""aff"":""How is your father?"",""ques"":""How are you feeling?""},{""person"":""Manner/way"",""aff"":""How does this work?"",""ques"":""How do I get there?""},{""person"":""Quality"",""aff"":""How was the movie?"",""ques"":""How did you like it?""}]}"
17: 1,"HOW OLD - Asking About Age",formula_table,Beginner,"Using HOW OLD to ask someone's age","{""formula"":""How old + be + subject?"",""affirmative"":""How old are you?"",""negative"":""How old isn't he?"",""question"":""How old is your brother?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Person"",""aff"":""How old is she?"",""ques"":""How old are your parents?""},{""person"":""Thing"",""aff"":""How old is this building?"",""ques"":""How old is your car?""},{""person"":""Answer format"",""aff"":""I am 25 years old. / I'm 25."",""ques"":""She's ten (years old).""}]}"
18: 1,"HOW MANY vs HOW MUCH",formula_table,Intermediate,"The critical difference between countable and uncountable nouns","{""formula"":""HOW MANY + plural countable noun? | HOW MUCH + uncountable noun?"",""affirmative"":""How many apples? / How much water?"",""negative"":""How many people aren't coming?"",""question"":""How many sisters do you have?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Countable (can count individually)"",""aff"":""apples, cars, books, people, ideas"",""ques"":""How many students are in the class?""},{""person"":""Uncountable (cannot count individually)"",""aff"":""water, sugar, money, time, information"",""ques"":""How much coffee do you drink?""},{""person"":""Price (HOW MUCH + no noun)"",""aff"":""How much is this shirt?"",""ques"":""How much does it cost?""}]}"
19: 1,"HOW OFTEN - Asking About Frequency",formula_table,Intermediate,"Using HOW OFTEN to ask how frequently something happens","{""formula"":""How often + do/does + subject + main verb?"",""affirmative"":""How often do you exercise?"",""negative"":""How often don't you sleep well?"",""question"":""How often does she travel?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Answer with frequency adverbs"",""aff"":""always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never"",""ques"":""I exercise twice a week.""},{""person"":""Answer with time expressions"",""aff"":""every day, once a week, three times a month"",""ques"":""She visits her parents every Sunday.""},{""person"":""Portuguese equivalent"",""aff"":""Com que frequência...?"",""ques"":""Quantas vezes...?""}]}"
20: 1,"HOW LONG - Asking About Duration",formula_table,Intermediate,"Using HOW LONG to ask about length of time","{""formula"":""How long + be + subject? (duration of an event) OR How long + does it take + to + verb? (time needed)"",""affirmative"":""How long is the movie?"",""negative"":""How long isn't the flight?"",""question"":""How long does it take to get there?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Duration of an event"",""aff"":""How long is the class?"",""ques"":""The movie is two hours long.""},{""person"":""Time needed for an action"",""aff"":""How long does it take to cook?"",""ques"":""It takes about 30 minutes.""},{""person"":""Answer format"",""aff"":""for two hours, since Monday, three days"",""ques"":""I've been waiting for an hour.""}]}"
21: 1,"HOW FAR - Asking About Distance",formula_table,Intermediate,"Using HOW FAR to ask about physical distance","{""formula"":""How far + be + subject + from + place?"",""affirmative"":""How far is your house from here?"",""negative"":""How far isn't the station?"",""question"":""How far do you live from work?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Distance in km/miles"",""aff"":""It's 10 kilometers away."",""ques"":""It's about 5 miles from here.""},{""person"":""Distance in time"",""aff"":""It's a 20-minute walk."",""ques"":""It takes 10 minutes by car.""},{""person"":""Portuguese equivalent"",""aff"":""Qual a distância? / Quão longe?"",""ques"":""Sua casa fica muito longe daqui?""}]}"
22: 1,"HOW COME - Informal WHY",usage_cases,Intermediate,"Using HOW COME for casual questions about reason","{""usage1"":""Informal replacement for WHY"",""example1a"":""How come you're late? = Why are you late?"",""example1b"":""How come she didn't come? = Why didn't she come?"",""usage2"":""Grammar difference - no auxiliary needed"",""example2a"":""WHY: Why are you sad? (need auxiliary 'are')"",""example2b"":""HOW COME: How come you're sad? (no extra auxiliary, just subject + verb)"",""teacher_note"":""'How come' is very common in everyday speech. It's less formal than 'why' and doesn't follow the normal question word order with auxiliary verbs.""}"
23: 1,"HOW ABOUT - Making Suggestions",usage_cases,Intermediate,"Using HOW ABOUT to offer or suggest something","{""usage1"":""Suggesting an activity"",""example1a"":""How about going to the cinema? = Que tal ir ao cinema?"",""example1b"":""How about we eat out tonight? = E se a gente jantar fora hoje?"",""usage2"":""Offering something"",""example2a"":""How about a coffee? = Que tal um café?"",""example2b"":""How about some help? = Aceita uma ajuda?"",""usage3"":""Form after HOW ABOUT"",""example3"":""How about + noun (How about a break?) OR How about + verb-ing (How about taking a break?)"",""teacher_note"":""Very common in spoken English for polite offers and suggestions. More natural than the formal 'Would you like to...?' in casual situations.""}"
24: 1,"The 5W-1H Method for Projects",deep_dive,Intermediate,"Asking all six questions to understand any situation completely","{""bullets"":[""WHAT = the topic, action, or object → What will we sell?"",""WHY = the reason or goal → Why open a coffee shop?"",""WHO = the people involved → Who will work there?"",""WHERE = the place or location → Where will it be?"",""WHEN = the time or timeline → When will it open?"",""HOW = the method or process → How will we make coffee?""],""explanation"":""In business, school projects, and news reporting, professionals use the 5W-1H method to make sure they understand 100% of a situation. Start with any question, then ask all six. You'll never miss an important detail again!""}"
25: 2,"The Probability Scale - Your Certainty Compass",formula_table,Beginner,"How sure are you? From 0% to 100%","{""formula"":""Modal verbs express different levels of certainty"",""affirmative"":""100% will / 95% must / 80% should / 60% could / 50% may / 30% might / 0% can't"",""negative"":""will not (won't) / must not (mustn't) / should not (shouldn't)"",""question"":""Will it rain? / Could he be right? / May I leave?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""100% Certain"",""aff"":""will"",""neg"":""won't"",""ques"":""Will it...?""},{""person"":""95% Certain (deduction)"",""aff"":""must"",""neg"":""can't"",""ques"":""Must it...?""},{""person"":""80% Likely"",""aff"":""should"",""neg"":""shouldn't"",""ques"":""Should it...?""},{""person"":""60% Possible"",""aff"":""could"",""neg"":""couldn't"",""ques"":""Could it...?""},{""person"":""50% Equal chance"",""aff"":""may"",""neg"":""may not"",""ques"":""May it...?""},{""person"":""30% Weak possibility"",""aff"":""might"",""neg"":""might not"",""ques"":""Might it...?""}]}"
26: 2,"The Three Golden Rules of Modal Verbs",deep_dive,Beginner,"Three rules with NO exceptions that govern all modal verbs","{""bullets"":[""Rule 1 - NO CONJUGATION: Modals never change for he/she/it. ❌ He cans swim. ✅ He can swim."",""Rule 2 - BASE FORM AFTER MODAL: No 'to', no -ing, no past tense. ❌ I must to go. ✅ I must go."",""Rule 3 - NO 'DO/DOES' IN QUESTIONS/NEGATIVES: Move the modal to the front. ❌ Do you can help? ✅ Can you help?""],""explanation"":""Modal verbs are like Lego bricks - they click into place without changing shape. Once you learn these three rules, you never have to worry about -s, -ed, or -ing endings with modals. This makes them easier than regular verbs in many ways!""}"
27: 2,"CAN - Ability, Permission, and Possibility",formula_table,Beginner,"All the ways to use CAN in English","{""formula"":""Subject + can + base verb"",""affirmative"":""I can swim. She can speak French."",""negative"":""I cannot (can't) swim. She can't drive."",""question"":""Can you help me? Can I borrow your pen?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Ability (saber/conseguir)"",""aff"":""I can play piano"",""neg"":""I can't sing"",""ques"":""Can you cook?""},{""person"":""Permission (informal)"",""aff"":""You can go now"",""neg"":""You can't smoke here"",""ques"":""Can I leave early?""},{""person"":""Request"",""ques"":""Can you pass the salt?""},{""person"":""Offer"",""ques"":""Can I help you with that?""},{""person"":""General possibility"",""aff"":""It can get cold here"",""neg"":""That can't be true"",""ques"":""Can this really happen?""}]}"
28: 2,"CAN - Common Mistakes for Portuguese Speakers",usage_cases,Intermediate,"Avoid these frequent errors when using CAN","{""usage1"":""Never use CAN for future specific guesses"",""example1a"":""❌ It can rain tomorrow. ✅ It may/might/could rain tomorrow."",""example1b"":""Can is for general truths: 'It can rain a lot in winter' (always true)"",""usage2"":""Never add 'to' after CAN"",""example2a"":""❌ I can to swim. ✅ I can swim."",""example2b"":""The structure is modal + base verb - no infinitive!"",""usage3"":""Never conjugate CAN"",""example3"":""❌ He cans speak. ✅ He can speak."",""teacher_note"":""In Portuguese, 'poder' covers many situations. English splits these into CAN, COULD, MAY, MIGHT. Always ask: Am I talking about ability, permission, or possibility?""}"
29: 2,"COULD - Polite Requests, Past Ability, Possibility",formula_table,Intermediate,"All the ways to use COULD in English","{""formula"":""Subject + could + base verb"",""affirmative"":""I could run fast when I was young. It could rain later."",""negative"":""I couldn't hear you. It couldn't be true."",""question"":""Could you help me? Could I borrow your car?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Polite request"",""ques"":""Could you open the window?""},{""person"":""Past ability"",""aff"":""When I was 5, I could swim"",""neg"":""I couldn't read until I was 7"",""ques"":""Could you speak English as a child?""},{""person"":""Possibility (weaker than can)"",""aff"":""He could be at home"",""neg"":""He couldn't have done it"",""ques"":""Could she be telling the truth?""},{""person"":""Suggestion"",""aff"":""You could try restarting your phone"",""neg"":""You couldn't do that"",""ques"":""Could we leave earlier?""},{""person"":""Conditional"",""aff"":""If I had money, I could buy a car"",""neg"":""I couldn't go if I wanted to"",""ques"":""Could you do it if you tried?""}]}"
30: 2,"CAN vs COULD - The Politeness and Probability Difference",deep_dive,Intermediate,"When to choose CAN versus COULD for requests and possibility","{""bullets"":[""REQUESTS - Politeness scale: CAN (40% casual) vs COULD (70% polite)"",""CAN you help me? (to a friend, direct)"",""COULD you help me? (to a stranger, more polite)"",""POSSIBILITY - Probability: CAN (80% likely as general truth) vs COULD (60% possible now)"",""'It can be dangerous here' = general truth (always dangerous)"",""'It could be dangerous tonight' = specific possibility (might be, might not)"",""PAST ABILITY: COULD for general past ability"",""'I could run fast when I was young' = many times, general ability"",""For one specific past achievement, use 'was able to' or 'managed to'"",""❌ It was hard but I could finish. ✅ It was hard but I managed to finish.""],""explanation"":""COULD is the softer, more polite, less certain version of CAN. Use it when you want to be more respectful or when you're less sure about something. In Brazilian Portuguese, 'poderia' is similar in politeness to 'could.'""}"
31: 2,"MAY - Formal Permission and 50/50 Possibility",formula_table,Intermediate,"All the ways to use MAY in English","{""formula"":""Subject + may + base verb"",""affirmative"":""I may go to the party. She may be late."",""negative"":""I may not have time. She may not come."",""question"":""May I leave early? May I come in?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Formal permission"",""aff"":""You may go now"",""neg"":""You may not smoke here"",""ques"":""May I speak to the manager?""},{""person"":""Possibility (50/50)"",""aff"":""We may go to the beach"",""neg"":""We may not have enough time"",""ques"":""May it be true?""},{""person"":""Wish/hope (fixed expressions)"",""aff"":""May you always be happy""}]}"
32: 2,"MAY vs MIGHT - The Probability Difference",deep_dive,Intermediate,"Understanding the subtle difference between MAY (50%) and MIGHT (30%)","{""bullets"":[""MAY = 50% chance - real possibility, equally likely to happen or not"",""MIGHT = 30% chance - weaker possibility, less likely to happen"",""'I may go to the party' = I haven't decided, 50/50 chance"",""'I might go to the party' = It's possible but unlikely (30%)"",""In everyday speech, many native speakers use MIGHT for both"",""But the formal difference is important for exams and precise writing"",""MAY is also more formal for permission than MIGHT"",""'May I leave?' (formal, classroom) vs 'Might I suggest...?' (extremely formal, rare)""],""explanation"":""Think of a coin flip for MAY (50/50). Think of rolling a die and needing a 6 for MIGHT (about 17% - even less likely). The difference is small but real. In Brazilian Portuguese, both often translate as 'pode ser que' or 'talvez.'""}"
33: 2,"MIGHT - Weak Possibility and Tentative Suggestions",formula_table,Intermediate,"All the ways to use MIGHT in English","{""formula"":""Subject + might + base verb"",""affirmative"":""I might come tomorrow. She might be stuck in traffic."",""negative"":""I might not have time. He might not remember."",""question"":""Might I suggest an alternative? (very formal)"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Weak possibility (30%)"",""aff"":""We might go to the cinema"",""neg"":""We might not have enough money"",""ques"":""Might it be true?""},{""person"":""Tentative suggestion"",""aff"":""You might want to apologise"",""neg"":""You might not want to say that""},{""person"":""Past possibility"",""aff"":""She might have missed the bus"",""neg"":""She might not have seen the message"",""ques"":""Might she have forgotten?""}]}"
34: 2,"SHOULD - Advice, Expectation, and Regret",formula_table,Intermediate,"All the ways to use SHOULD in English","{""formula"":""Subject + should + base verb"",""affirmative"":""You should see a doctor. The train should arrive soon."",""negative"":""You shouldn't eat so much sugar. He shouldn't be late."",""question"":""Should I call her? Should we wait?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Advice / Recommendation"",""aff"":""You should study more"",""neg"":""You shouldn't smoke"",""ques"":""Should I take the job?""},{""person"":""Expectation (80% sure)"",""aff"":""He should be home by now"",""neg"":""She shouldn't be long"",""ques"":""Should the bus be here yet?""},{""person"":""Past regret (should have)"",""aff"":""I should have studied harder"",""neg"":""I shouldn't have said that"",""ques"":""Should I have told her?""}]}"
35: 2,"SHOULD vs MUST - Advice vs Obligation",deep_dive,Intermediate,"Understanding the strength difference between SHOULD and MUST","{""bullets"":[""SHOULD = recommendation, advice (80% strength) - you have a choice"",""MUST = obligation, necessity (100% strength) - no choice, it's required"",""'You should see a doctor' = I recommend it, but you decide"",""'You must wear a seatbelt' = It's the law, you have no choice"",""For logical deduction: MUST is 95% certain, SHOULD is 80% certain"",""'The lights are on - they must be home' (very sure)"",""'The train should arrive at 5 PM' (based on schedule, but delays possible)"",""Negative forms: shouldn't (advice against) vs must not (prohibition, forbidden)"",""'You shouldn't eat late' (advice) vs 'You must not enter' (you're not allowed)""],""explanation"":""Think of SHOULD as a strong suggestion and MUST as a command. In Brazilian Portuguese, 'você deveria' matches SHOULD, and 'você tem que' or 'você precisa' matches MUST.""}"
36: 2,"MUST - Obligation and Logical Deduction",formula_table,Intermediate,"All the ways to use MUST in English","{""formula"":""Subject + must + base verb"",""affirmative"":""You must wear a helmet. She must be tired - she worked all night."",""negative"":""You must not (mustn't) touch that wire."",""question"":""Must I finish today? (rare - usually 'Do I have to?')"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Obligation (law/rule)"",""aff"":""Employees must wear ID"",""neg"":""You must not smoke here"",""ques"":""Must we arrive early?""},{""person"":""Logical deduction (95% sure)"",""aff"":""She's crying - she must be sad"",""neg"":""That can't be true - he's in Tokyo"",""ques"":""Must it be this way?""},{""person"":""Strong internal need"",""aff"":""I must finish this report"",""neg"":""I must not forget""}]}"
37: 2,"MUST vs HAVE TO - Obligation from Inside vs Outside",deep_dive,Intermediate,"Understanding where the obligation comes from","{""bullets"":[""MUST = internal obligation (I feel I need to do this) OR very strong external rules"",""HAVE TO = external obligation (someone/something else requires it)"",""'I must finish this project' = I feel personally that I need to do it"",""'I have to finish this project' = my boss requires it, the deadline is tomorrow"",""In everyday speech, HAVE TO is much more common for external obligations"",""MUST is often too strong for casual conversation"",""The biggest trap: MUSTN'T vs DON'T HAVE TO"",""MUSTN'T = it is forbidden (proibido)"",""DON'T HAVE TO = it is not necessary (não precisa)"",""❌ You mustn't pay for the ticket (sounds like it's illegal to pay)"",""✅ You don't have to pay for the ticket (it's free - no need to pay)""],""explanation"":""This is one of the most common mistakes for Portuguese speakers! In Portuguese, 'não pode' can mean both 'you must not' (forbidden) and 'you don't have to' (not necessary). English separates these completely. MUSTN'T = forbidden. DON'T HAVE TO = not necessary.""}"
38: 2,"WILL - Certainty, Promises, and Spontaneous Decisions",formula_table,Beginner,"All the ways to use WILL in English","{""formula"":""Subject + will + base verb"",""affirmative"":""I will call you tomorrow. It will rain."",""negative"":""I will not (won't) forget. She won't be late."",""question"":""Will you help me? Will it be sunny?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Future action (100% certain)"",""aff"":""The sun will rise at 6:15"",""neg"":""It won't snow in July"",""ques"":""Will you be there?""},{""person"":""Promise"",""aff"":""I will always love you"",""neg"":""I will never forget""},{""person"":""Spontaneous decision"",""aff"":""The phone's ringing - I'll get it"",""neg"":""I won't answer""},{""person"":""Offer"",""aff"":""I'll carry those bags for you""}]}"
39: 2,"WILL vs GOING TO - Predicting vs Planning",deep_dive,Intermediate,"Understanding the difference between WILL (prediction) and GOING TO (plan/evidence)","{""bullets"":[""WILL = prediction based on opinion, spontaneous decision, promise"",""GOING TO = plan made before speaking, prediction based on present evidence"",""'I think it will rain tomorrow' = opinion, guess"",""'Look at those dark clouds - it's going to rain' = evidence (you see the clouds)"",""'The phone is ringing - I'll get it' = spontaneous decision (just decided now)"",""'I'm going to call my mom later' = plan made before (already decided)"",""Promise: always WILL - 'I will never tell anyone'"",""For predictions without evidence, both are possible with slight nuance""],""explanation"":""This difference is subtle but important. WILL is for instant decisions, promises, and predictions based on what you think. GOING TO is for planned actions and predictions based on what you can see. In Brazilian Portuguese, both often translate to 'vou' which makes this difficult - pay attention to whether there's evidence or a pre-existing plan!""}"
40: 2,"WOULD - Polite Offers, Habits, and Hypotheticals",formula_table,Intermediate,"All the ways to use WOULD in English","{""formula"":""Subject + would + base verb"",""affirmative"":""I would like some coffee. When I was young, I would play outside."",""negative"":""I wouldn't do that if I were you. She wouldn't say that."",""question"":""Would you like some tea? Would you mind closing the door?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Polite offer/request"",""ques"":""Would you like help? / Would you mind waiting?""},{""person"":""Past habit"",""aff"":""We would visit grandma every Sunday"",""neg"":""I wouldn't eat vegetables as a child"",""ques"":""Would you play there often?""},{""person"":""Unreal conditional"",""aff"":""If I were rich, I would travel"",""neg"":""I wouldn't do that if I were you"",""ques"":""Would you go if you could?""},{""person"":""Reported speech"",""aff"":""He said he would come"",""neg"":""She said she wouldn't be late""}]}"
41: 2,"WOULD for Hypotheticals - The Unreal Conditional",deep_dive,Intermediate,"Using WOULD to talk about imaginary situations","{""bullets"":[""Unreal conditional = IF + past simple, WOULD + base verb"",""The situation is NOT real - it's imaginary or impossible"",""'If I were rich, I would buy a yacht' (but I'm not rich)"",""'If I were you, I would apologise' (I can't be you - giving advice)"",""'What would you do if you won the lottery?' (imagining a future possibility)"",""Notice: we use WERE for all persons in the IF clause (even I/he/she)"",""'If I were...' not 'If I was...' (though some native speakers say 'was' in casual speech)"",""This is different from the first conditional (possible situations):"",""First conditional: 'If it rains, I will stay home' (possible, 50% chance)"",""Second conditional (unreal): 'If I won the lottery, I would travel' (unlikely, imaginary)""],""explanation"":""WOULD doesn't fit on the probability scale because it describes imaginary or habitual situations, not real predictions about the future. Think of WOULD as the past or imaginary version of WILL.""}"
42: 2,"Permission and Politeness - The Politeness Ladder",formula_table,Intermediate,"How to ask for permission from casual to ultra-polite","{""formula"":""Politeness level determines which modal to use"",""affirmative"":""Can (40% casual) / Could (60% polite) / May (70% formal) / Would you mind (80% ultra-polite)"",""negative"":""Can't (direct refusal) / Couldn't (polite refusal) / May not (formal refusal)"",""question"":""Can I use your phone? / Could I leave early? / May I speak to the manager?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Casual (friends, family)"",""aff"":""Can I borrow £5?"",""neg"":""You can't go"",""ques"":""Can I sit here?""},{""person"":""Polite (strangers, colleagues)"",""aff"":""Could I borrow £5?"",""neg"":""You couldn't possibly"",""ques"":""Could I have a moment?""},{""person"":""Formal (professional, official)"",""aff"":""May I borrow £5?"",""neg"":""You may not"",""ques"":""May I come in?""},{""person"":""Ultra-polite (very respectful)"",""aff"":""Would you mind if I borrowed £5?"",""ques"":""Would you mind if I sat here?""}]}"
43: 2,"Advice - How Strongly to Recommend",usage_cases,Intermediate,"Choosing between COULD, SHOULD, and MUST for giving advice","{""usage1"":""COULD (40% strength) - gentle option, just a suggestion"",""example1a"":""You could try yoga for back pain"",""example1b"":""We could eat at that new restaurant"",""usage2"":""SHOULD (80% strength) - strong recommendation, expectation"",""example2a"":""You should see a dentist about that tooth"",""example2b"":""You should apologise to her"",""usage3"":""MUST (100% strength) - obligation, no choice, command"",""example3a"":""You must stop smoking - it's killing you"",""example3b"":""You must wear a helmet on this site"",""teacher_note"":""In Brazilian Portuguese, 'você poderia' (could), 'você deveria' (should), and 'você tem que' (must) follow the same strength pattern. Match the strength to the situation!""}"
44: 3,"Modal Perfect - Talking About the Past",formula_table,Intermediate,"How to express past possibility, regret, and deduction using modal + have + past participle","{""formula"":""Modal + have + past participle (V3)"",""affirmative"":""I should have studied. She must have left."",""negative"":""I shouldn't have said that. They couldn't have known."",""question"":""Should I have called? Could she have forgotten?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Regret / Past advice not followed"",""aff"":""I should have studied"",""neg"":""I shouldn't have eaten so much"",""ques"":""Should I have told her?""},{""person"":""Past ability not used"",""aff"":""We could have won"",""neg"":""We couldn't have beaten them"",""ques"":""Could they have escaped?""},{""person"":""Past weak possibility"",""aff"":""She might have missed the train"",""neg"":""He might not have seen the message"",""ques"":""Might she have taken the wrong bus?""},{""person"":""Strong past deduction (95%)"",""aff"":""He must have left early - his car is gone"",""neg"":""They can't have arrived yet - it's too early""}]}"
45: 3,"SHOULD HAVE - Expressing Regret",deep_dive,Intermediate,"Using SHOULD HAVE to talk about past mistakes and regrets","{""bullets"":[""SHOULD HAVE = past advice that wasn't followed = regret"",""'I should have studied more for the test' (but I didn't - now I regret it)"",""'She should have called me' (she didn't - I'm upset about it)"",""SHOULDN'T HAVE = past action that was a bad idea = negative regret"",""'I shouldn't have eaten that much pizza' (I ate it - now I feel sick)"",""'You shouldn't have told her my secret' (you told her - now everyone knows)"",""In Brazilian Portuguese: 'eu deveria ter feito' matches this exactly"",""Common mistake: using the simple past instead of the past participle"",""❌ I should have went to the doctor"",""✅ I should have gone to the doctor"",""❌ She should have called me"",""✅ She should have called me (called is correct - it's the past participle)""],""explanation"":""The modal perfect is how English expresses possibility, ability, and regret in the past. The structure is always MODAL + HAVE + PAST PARTICIPLE. The past participle never changes - the modal tells you the time and meaning.""}"
46: 3,"COULD HAVE - Lost Opportunities",deep_dive,Intermediate,"Using COULD HAVE to talk about past possibilities that didn't happen","{""bullets"":[""COULD HAVE = something was possible in the past, but it didn't happen"",""'You could have asked me for help' (you didn't ask - but I was available)"",""'We could have won the game' (we lost - but it was possible to win)"",""'I could have been a doctor' (I chose a different career - but I had the ability)"",""COULDN'T HAVE = something was impossible in the past"",""'She couldn't have stolen the money - she was with me all day' (impossible)"",""'He couldn't have known about the surprise party' (he didn't know - impossible)"",""Common in excuses and explanations: 'I could have called, but my phone died'"",""Different from WOULD HAVE (conditional outcome) and MIGHT HAVE (less certain)""],""explanation"":""COULD HAVE is about unrealized potential. The ability existed, but the action didn't happen. In Brazilian Portuguese: 'eu poderia ter feito' (but I didn't).""}"
47: 4,"Simple Present - When to Use It",usage_cases,Beginner,"The four main uses of Simple Present tense","{""usage1"":""Habits and Routines (ações habituais)"",""example1a"":""I wake up at 7 AM every day."",""example1b"":""She drinks coffee every morning."",""usage2"":""General Truths and Facts (verdades universais)"",""example2a"":""Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius."",""example2b"":""The Earth revolves around the Sun."",""usage3"":""Fixed Schedules (horários fixos)"",""example3a"":""The train leaves at 6 PM."",""example3b"":""The movie starts at 8:30."",""usage4"":""Narrative Present (resumo de filmes/livros)"",""example4"":""In the final chapter, the detective discovers the truth.""}"
48: 4,"Simple Present - Formation Rules",formula_table,Beginner,"How to form affirmative, negative, and question sentences","{""formula"":""Subject + base verb (+ -s for he/she/it)"",""affirmative"":""I work / You work / He works / She works / It works / We work / They work"",""negative"":""I don't work / He doesn't work / They don't work"",""question"":""Do you work? / Does she work? / Do they work?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""I"",""aff"":""I work"",""neg"":""I don't work"",""ques"":""Do I work?""},{""person"":""You"",""aff"":""You work"",""neg"":""You don't work"",""ques"":""Do you work?""},{""person"":""He/She/It"",""aff"":""He works / She works / It works"",""neg"":""He doesn't work"",""ques"":""Does he work?""},{""person"":""We"",""aff"":""We work"",""neg"":""We don't work"",""ques"":""Do we work?""},{""person"":""They"",""aff"":""They work"",""neg"":""They don't work"",""ques"":""Do they work?""}]}"
49: 4,"Simple Present - Spelling Rules for -s/-es",deep_dive,Beginner,"How to add -s or -es to verbs for he/she/it","{""bullets"":[""Most verbs: just add -s (eat → eats, read → reads)"",""Verbs ending in -ss, -sh, -ch, -x, -o: add -es (kiss → kisses, wash → washes, watch → watches, fix → fixes, go → goes)"",""Verbs ending in consonant + -y: change y to i and add -es (study → studies, cry → cries, fly → flies)"",""Verbs ending in vowel + -y: just add -s (play → plays, enjoy → enjoys, say → says)"",""Common irregulars: have → has, do → does, be → is""],""explanation"":""The third person -s is the only conjugation in Simple Present. Unlike Portuguese, you don't change the verb for each person - only for he/she/it. This makes English simpler!""}"
50: 4,"Present Continuous - When to Use It",usage_cases,Beginner,"The five main uses of Present Continuous tense","{""usage1"":""Action happening RIGHT NOW (no momento da fala)"",""example1a"":""I am reading this explanation right now."",""example1b"":""Look! It is snowing outside."",""usage2"":""Temporary situation (situação temporária)"",""example2a"":""I am staying with a friend this week."",""example2b"":""She is reading a great book at the moment."",""usage3"":""Changing/developing situation (tendências)"",""example3a"":""The cost of living is increasing rapidly."",""example3b"":""My English is getting better every day."",""usage4"":""Future arrangement (plano fixo para o futuro)"",""example4a"":""We are meeting the clients at 7:30 tomorrow."",""example4b"":""I am flying to São Paulo next Monday."",""usage5"":""Annoyance with 'always' (crítica com 'sempre')"",""example5"":""He is always losing his keys! (irritation)""}"
51: 4,"Present Continuous - Formation Rules",formula_table,Beginner,"How to form affirmative, negative, and question sentences with -ing","{""formula"":""Subject + am/is/are + verb-ing"",""affirmative"":""I am working / You are working / He is working / She is working / It is working / We are working / They are working"",""negative"":""I am not working / He isn't working / They aren't working"",""question"":""Am I working? / Is she working? / Are they working?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""I"",""aff"":""I am working"",""neg"":""I am not working"",""ques"":""Am I working?""},{""person"":""You"",""aff"":""You are working"",""neg"":""You aren't working"",""ques"":""Are you working?""},{""person"":""He/She/It"",""aff"":""He is working"",""neg"":""He isn't working"",""ques"":""Is he working?""},{""person"":""We"",""aff"":""We are working"",""neg"":""We aren't working"",""ques"":""Are we working?""},{""person"":""They"",""aff"":""They are working"",""neg"":""They aren't working"",""ques"":""Are they working?""}]}"
52: 4,"Present Continuous - Spelling Rules for -ING",deep_dive,Beginner,"How to add -ing to verbs correctly","{""bullets"":[""Most verbs: just add -ing (eat → eating, read → reading)"",""Verbs ending in silent -e: drop the -e, add -ing (make → making, dance → dancing, write → writing)"",""Exception: verbs ending in -ee: keep both (see → seeing, agree → agreeing)"",""CVC (consonant-vowel-consonant) one-syllable: double last consonant (sit → sitting, run → running, stop → stopping)"",""Verbs ending in -ie: change to -y (lie → lying, die → dying, tie → tying)"",""Two-syllable verbs: double if stress is on last syllable (begin → beginning, but open → opening)""],""explanation"":""The -ing form is also called the present participle. It's used not only in Present Continuous but also after verbs like 'enjoy', 'finish', and 'mind' (I enjoy swimming, I finished reading).""}"
53: 4,"Simple Present vs Present Continuous - The Critical Difference",deep_dive,Intermediate,"Understanding when to use each tense, especially for Portuguese speakers","{""bullets"":[""SIMPLE PRESENT = permanent, habitual, general truth (permanente, habitual, verdade geral)"",""PRESENT CONTINUOUS = temporary, in progress, happening now (temporário, em progresso, acontecendo agora)"",""Portuguese uses the same tense for both: 'Eu estudo inglês' (habitual) and 'Eu estudo inglês agora' (in progress)"",""English forces you to choose: 'I study English' (habitual) vs 'I am studying English now' (in progress)"",""Compare these pairs:"",""'I work at a bank' = permanent job (Simple Present)"",""'I am working on a special project' = temporary (Present Continuous)"",""'She lives in London' = permanent home (Simple Present)"",""'She is staying with friends' = temporary (Present Continuous)"",""'He always loses his keys' = neutral fact (Simple Present)"",""'He is always losing his keys' = complaint/annoyance (Present Continuous)""],""explanation"":""This is the biggest difference between English and Portuguese present tenses! In Portuguese, you use the same tense for both. In English, you MUST choose based on whether something is permanent/habitual (Simple) or temporary/in progress (Continuous).""}"
54: 5,"Stative Verbs - The Exception to Continuous",formula_table,Intermediate,"Verbs that describe states, not actions - never use them in continuous form","{""formula"":""Stative verbs = NO -ing form. Use Simple Present instead."",""affirmative"":""I know the answer. (NOT: I am knowing)"",""negative"":""I don't understand. (NOT: I am not understanding)"",""question"":""Do you believe me? (NOT: Are you believing?)"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""Mental states (thoughts)"",""aff"":""I know / believe / understand / remember / think (opinion)"",""neg"":""I don't remember"",""ques"":""Do you understand?""},{""person"":""Emotional states (feelings)"",""aff"":""I love / hate / like / want / need / prefer"",""neg"":""I don't like it"",""ques"":""Do you want some?""},{""person"":""Sensory perception"",""aff"":""I see / hear / smell / taste / feel"",""neg"":""I don't see it"",""ques"":""Do you hear that?""},{""person"":""Possession"",""aff"":""I have (own) / belong / own / possess"",""neg"":""I don't have it"",""ques"":""Do you have a car?""},{""person"":""State of being"",""aff"":""I am / seem / appear / cost / weigh"",""neg"":""It doesn't cost much"",""ques"":""Does it seem right?""}]}"
55: 5,"Stative Verb Categories - Complete List",usage_cases,Intermediate,"Five categories of stative verbs with detailed examples","{""usage1"":""Mental States (cognition, belief)"",""example1a"":""know, believe, understand, remember, forget, think (opinion)"",""example1b"":""mean, suppose, realize, recognize, doubt"",""usage2"":""Emotional States (feelings, desire)"",""example2a"":""like, love, hate, want, need, prefer, wish, desire"",""example2b"":""appreciate, care, envy, fear, forgive, respect"",""usage3"":""Sensory Perception (involuntary senses)"",""example3a"":""see, hear, smell, taste, feel (involuntary)"",""example3b"":""'I see a bird' vs 'I am seeing the doctor' (different meaning!)"",""usage4"":""Possession and Relationship"",""example4a"":""have (possess), own, possess, belong, include, contain"",""example4b"":""consist of, depend on, concern, involve"",""usage5"":""State of Being and Appearance"",""example5a"":""be, seem, appear, look (appearance), sound, resemble"",""example5b"":""cost, weigh, measure, equal""}"
56: 5,"Dynamic Verbs - The Exceptions (Two Meanings)",deep_dive,Intermediate,"Verbs that can be stative OR dynamic depending on meaning","{""bullets"":[""THINK - stative (opinion): 'I think it's a good idea' (NO -ing)"",""THINK - dynamic (mental process): 'I am thinking about the question' (-ing OK)"",""HAVE - stative (possess): 'She has a car' (NO -ing)"",""HAVE - dynamic (experience/consume): 'She is having lunch / a shower' (-ing OK)"",""SEE - stative (sight): 'I see a rainbow' (NO -ing)"",""SEE - dynamic (meet): 'I am seeing the doctor tomorrow' (-ing OK)"",""TASTE - stative (flavor): 'This milk tastes sour' (NO -ing)"",""TASTE - dynamic (sample): 'The chef is tasting the sauce' (-ing OK)"",""SMELL - stative (odor): 'The flowers smell lovely' (NO -ing)"",""SMELL - dynamic (sniff): 'The dog is smelling the ground' (-ing OK)"",""FEEL - stative (texture): 'Velvet feels soft' (NO -ing)"",""FEEL - dynamic (touch/emotion): 'I am feeling the fabric / much better' (-ing OK)""],""explanation"":""These verbs are tricky because their meaning changes. When they describe a state (opinion, possession, involuntary perception), use Simple Present. When they describe an action (mental process, experience, voluntary action), you can use Present Continuous.""}"
57: 5,"The Verb ENJOY - Special Case",usage_cases,Intermediate,"Why ENJOY is different from other emotion verbs","{""usage1"":""Although ENJOY describes a feeling, it is DYNAMIC"",""example1a"":""I enjoy playing tennis. (Simple Present for general fact)"",""example1b"":""I am enjoying this party very much. (Present Continuous for current experience)"",""usage2"":""Compare with other emotion verbs"",""example2a"":""I love this song. (stative - NO continuous: ❌ I am loving)"",""example2b"":""I am enjoying this song. (dynamic - continuous OK)"",""teacher_note"":""Because 'enjoy' implies ACTIVE, conscious appreciation of an ongoing experience, it works in the continuous form. 'Love' and 'like' are passive states - they don't take continuous.""}"
58: 5,"Special ""To Be"" - Permanent vs Temporary Behavior",deep_dive,Intermediate,"The only exception: using ""being"" to show temporary, deliberate behavior","{""bullets"":[""To Be is normally stative (never continuous): 'She is a doctor' (NOT: She is being a doctor)"",""ONE EXCEPTION: am/is/are + being + adjective = temporary BEHAVIOR (not permanent characteristic)"",""'He is rude' = permanent personality - he is always rude"",""'He is being rude' = temporary behavior - he is acting rude NOW (maybe not usually)"",""WHEN TO USE: only for CONTROLLABLE BEHAVIORS (actions the person chooses)"",""Permissible adjectives: nice, helpful, patient, silly, rude, difficult, lazy, noisy, quiet"",""NOT permissible (permanent qualities): tall, short, fat, thin, beautiful, old, young, intelligent, stupid"",""❌ He is being tall (height is not a behavior you can choose)"",""❌ The car is being red (color is a state, not behavior)"",""Examples: 'Why are you being so quiet tonight?' (unusual behavior)"",""'Stop it - you are being silly!' (acting silly now)""],""explanation"":""This is an advanced nuance that separates intermediate from advanced speakers. It signals that you understand that BEING describes temporary, chosen behavior, not permanent identity. Very useful for workplace and social situations!""}"
59: 6,"Simple Past - Regular Verbs (Rule 1: Add -D)",formula_table,Beginner,"Verbs ending in silent -e: just add -D","{""formula"":""Base verb + -d (for verbs ending in -e)"",""affirmative"":""love → loved / like → liked / dance → danced"",""negative"":""did not love / didn't like / didn't dance"",""question"":""Did you love it? / Did she like it? / Did they dance?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""love"",""aff"":""loved"",""neg"":""didn't love"",""ques"":""Did you love?""},{""person"":""like"",""aff"":""liked"",""neg"":""didn't like"",""ques"":""Did she like?""},{""person"":""dance"",""aff"":""danced"",""neg"":""didn't dance"",""ques"":""Did they dance?""}]}"
60: 6,"Simple Past - Regular Verbs (Rule 2: Add -ED)",formula_table,Beginner,"Most verbs: just add -ED","{""formula"":""Base verb + -ed"",""affirmative"":""work → worked / need → needed / help → helped"",""negative"":""did not work / didn't need / didn't help"",""question"":""Did you work? / Did she need? / Did they help?"",""conjugations"":[{""person"":""work"",""aff"":""worked"",""neg"":""didn't work"",""ques"":""Did you work?""},{""person"":""need"",""aff"":""needed"",""neg"":""didn't need"",""ques"":""Did she need?""},{""person"":""help"",""aff"":""helped"",""neg"":""didn't help"",""ques"":""Did they help?""}]}"
61: 6,"Simple Past - Spelling Rule: Consonant + Y → -IED",deep_dive,Beginner,"Change Y to I and add -ED for verbs ending in consonant + Y","{""bullets"":[""Rule: consonant + Y → change Y to I, add -ED"",""study → studied (NOT studyed)"",""cry → cried (NOT cryed)"",""try → tried (NOT tryed)"",""reply → replied"",""If the Y comes after a VOWEL, just add -ED (no change):"",""play → played, enjoy → enjoyed, stay → stayed"",""This is exactly like Portuguese: estudar → estudou (the Y becomes I)"",""Common mistake: 'I tryed my best' ❌ → 'I tried my best' ✅""],""explanation"":""English spelling changes to maintain pronunciation. When a verb ends in consonant + Y, the Y becomes I to keep the vowel sound consistent. Verbs ending in vowel + Y keep the Y because the vowel sound is already clear.""}"
62: 6,"Simple Past - CVC Rule (Double Consonant)",deep_dive,Intermediate,"One-syllable CVC verbs double the final consonant before adding -ED","{""bullets"":[""CVC = Consonant - Vowel - Consonant pattern"",""stop → stopped (double the P)"",""plan → planned (double the N)"",""rub → rubbed (double the B)"",""Do NOT double if the verb ends in W, X, or Y:"",""snow → snowed (W = no double)"",""fix → fixed (X = no double)"",""play → played (Y = no double)"",""For two-syllable verbs: double only if the LAST syllable is stressed"",""preFER → preferred (stress on second syllable)"",""ocCUR → occurred (stress on second syllable)"",""LISten → listened (stress on first syllable → no double)"",""TRAvel → traveled (US) / travelled (UK)"",""Common mistake: 'He stoped the car' ❌ → 'He stopped the car' ✅""],""explanation"":""We double the consonant to keep the vowel sound short. Without doubling, 'stopped' would rhyme with 'hoped' - completely different pronunciation. This rule protects the original vowel sound.""}"

</content>

<path>/home/leonardo/Documents/Personal/Inglês/Aulas/materialLeo/ankiCards/flashcards/import/CSV_STRUCTURE_FOR_AI.md</path>
